query: derived state anti pattern react interview question

selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: No exact-topic GreatFrontEnd page was confirmed for this React prompt, so broader React question-bank pages would be weaker competitors than topic-direct sources.

competitors.label: Choosing the State Structure
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical React page for avoiding redundant and duplicated state, including the official warning against mirroring props into state unnecessarily.
theirStrengths: It is the authoritative React explanation of redundant state, duplicate state, and the official 'don't mirror props in state' guidance.
theirGaps: It is broader than this single interview topic and less compressed around the exact derived-state anti-pattern framing.

realWorldUseCases: Derived state usually means you copied something React could already compute from props or other state. That creates two sources of truth
Your UI becomes inconsistent: it shows old computed values from a previous render.
followUpConfusion: If a value can be computed from existing inputs, storing it as state means you must keep it manually synced.
Most of the time: compute/memoize is simpler and safer.

realWorldUseCases: The docs explain redundant state well, but our page is more direct on the production symptom pattern: refetches, websocket updates, and stale computed values.
actionableExamples: The official examples are strong, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is clearer on the narrow exceptions where derived state is still justified, such as drafts and snapshots with explicit resync rules.

actionableExamples: Keep one tiny before-and-after example showing useEffect(setDerived) versus compute-in-render so the anti-pattern stays concrete.

competitors.winReason: The official docs are authoritative, but our page is stronger for interview use because it turns redundant state guidance into a sharper derive-versus-store decision.

competitors.label: State Derivation
competitors.selectionReason: This tutorial is a strong topic-direct competitor because it focuses exactly on not storing values that can be derived from props or other state.
theirStrengths: It is tightly scoped to derived-state drift and the compute-or-memoize alternative.
theirGaps: It is more rule-oriented and less explicit about the interview-grade edge case where a local draft is intentionally allowed not to track the source.

realWorldUseCases: If items changes (refetch, filtering, WebSocket update), count won’t automatically update
The bug is rarely obvious at first: one path updates, the other drifts, and the UI becomes stale.
followUpConfusion: It’s acceptable when you intentionally want a snapshot or a locally editable copy that should not always follow the source automatically.
If you want to reset draft on user change → set draft when the identity changes (not every render).

realWorldUseCases: The style guide is strong on stale derived totals and drift, but our page is stronger because it also handles the justified exceptions and their resync rules.
actionableExamples: Its correct-versus-incorrect examples are strong, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is clearer on the likely follow-up that 'editable draft' is not the same as 'mirror every prop change into state'.

actionableExamples: Add one explicit profile-switching draft example later if we want the reset-versus-preserve decision to be even more concrete.

competitors.winReason: This tutorial is directly on topic, but our page is stronger because it pairs the anti-pattern warning with the narrow cases where derived state is actually acceptable.

competitors.label: Is using two states in React an anti-pattern when one state can entirely determine the other state?
competitors.selectionReason: This community discussion is a direct practical reference for redundant derived state and the extra rerenders and synchronization problems it creates.
theirStrengths: It states the derived-state anti-pattern plainly and connects it to redundant rerenders and unnecessary synchronization.
theirGaps: It is narrower than our page on copied props, stale computed values, memoization as the safer alternative, and justified draft exceptions.

followUpConfusion: If you ever write useEffect(() => setX(deriveFromY), [y]), pause and ask:
Prefer computing derived values during render or with useMemo.

realWorldUseCases: The thread explains why redundant state causes extra work, but our page is stronger because it covers the broader failure modes and how they show up in real screens.
actionableExamples: Its simplified state example is still useful, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is clearer on when derived state is still valid and how to decide reset-versus-preserve behavior for local drafts.

actionableExamples: Keep one explicit useEffect(setDerived) anti-pattern example visible so the community-style shortcut remains easy to spot.

competitors.winReason: The community thread correctly flags redundant state, but our page is stronger because it explains both the anti-pattern and the narrow legitimate uses.
